steps.prompt: Which word is used as a vocative address?
What is the subject of 'est' in this sentence?
steps.reveal: nos hic esse = for us to be here (subject infinitive)

hints.Magister: 'Magister' is vocative — not the grammatical subject
hints.bonum: 'Bonum' is the predicate adjective — not the subject
hints.hic: 'Hic' is an adverb modifying 'esse' — not the subject by itself

hints.to eat: That would be 'edere' — 'est' here is from 'esse' (to be)
hints.to stand: That would be 'stare' — 'est' is from 'esse' (to be)
hints.to remain: That would be 'manere' — 'est' is from 'esse' (to be)

hints.present subjunctive: Present subjunctive would be 'sit' — 'est' is present indicative
hints.perfect indicative: Perfect would be 'fuit' — 'est' is present indicative
hints.imperfect indicative: Imperfect would be 'erat' — 'est' is present indicative

hints.edo, esse, edi, esum: These are the parts of 'edere' (to eat)
hints.sto, stare, steti, statum: These are the parts of 'stare' (to stand)
hints.sisto, sistere, stiti, statum: These are the parts of 'sistere' (to cause to stand)

hints.nominative: While 'nos' looks identical in nominative, as subject of an infinitive it is accusative
hints.ablative: Ablative would also be 'nobis' — 'nos' here is accusative subject of the infinitive
hints.dative: Dative would be 'nobis' — 'nos' is accusative subject of infinitive

hints.accusative: While 'bonum' is identical in accusative neuter, here it is predicate with 'est'
hints.ablative: Ablative neuter would be 'bono' — 'bonum' is nominative predicate
hints.vocative: Vocative would be for direct address — 'bonum' is predicate adjective

hints.1st declension: 1st declension has genitive in -ae — 'magistri' ends in -i
hints.3rd declension: 3rd declension has genitive in -is — 'magistri' ends in -i
hints.4th declension: 4th declension has genitive in -ūs — 'magistri' ends in -i

steps.prompt: What is the grammatical function of the infinitive 'esse'?
steps.target: subject infinitive (with 'nos' as its accusative subject)
choices: subject infinitive (with 'nos' as its accusative subject)

hints.complementary infinitive: A complementary infinitive completes verbs like 'volo' — here 'esse' is the subject of 'est'
hints.purpose infinitive: Purpose is expressed by 'ut' + subjunctive — 'esse' is the subject of 'bonum est'
hints.indirect statement: Indirect statement requires a verb of saying/thinking — 'bonum est' is impersonal

translation: Master, it is good for us to be here
